pattern: Every task must land whole inside some work session, so the problem is really about partitioning the task set into the fewest groups whose sums each fit the session budget - a bitmask subset-cover search over which tasks share a session.
hints: A task can never be split across two sessions, so think in terms of grouping the whole set of tasks into sessions rather than scheduling them one at a time.
For every subset of tasks, precompute whether that subset's total time fits in one session; then the answer is the fewest such subsets needed to cover everyone.
Build a table over all 2^n subsets: the minimum sessions to finish a subset is one plus the minimum over any of its fitting sub-subsets for the rest, so iterate over sub-masks of each mask.

edge_inputs.description: three tasks, two share a session
five tasks all fit one long session
single task exactly equal to the session time
three equal tasks that each already fill the session alone
four tasks that pair up perfectly two per session
max task count, all tiny, one session suffices
max task count, each task saturates the session alone
a small filler task only fits with one particular neighbor

stress.description: random task sets near the max subset-search size
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = min(size, 14)
    session_time = rng.randint(5, 15)
    tasks = [rng.randint(1, session_time) for _ in range(n)]
    return [tasks, session_time]
